title: Cat Retained Baby Teeth: Guide to 'Double Fangs' in Kittens
meta_desc: Learn about retained baby teeth in cats, why double fangs occur, and how to triage your kitten's dental health effectively.
content_html:
TL;DR: Retained baby teeth occur when a kitten's deciduous teeth fail to fall out as adult teeth emerge, requiring a non-emergency veterinary visit to prevent long-term dental issues.

What are retained baby teeth in kittens and how do they affect my pet?

Retained deciduous teeth, commonly known as 'double fangs,' occur when a kitten's baby teeth fail to fall out as the permanent adult teeth emerge. This most frequently happens with the canine teeth, resulting in two teeth occupying the same socket. This condition can lead to overcrowding, trapped food, and potential misalignment of the permanent bite.

Is it an emergency if my kitten has retained baby teeth?

  • The urgency level for retained baby teeth is Low.
  • While not an immediate emergency, this condition requires a scheduled veterinary consultation.
  • If the baby tooth remains too long, it can cause the adult tooth to grow incorrectly, leading to pain or periodontal disease.
  • Most veterinarians recommend addressing this during your kitten's spay or neuter surgery.

How does sending a photo help the vet triage my kitten's teeth?

  • A clear photograph allows the veterinary team to see the proximity of the two teeth.
  • Professionals can check for signs of gum inflammation or abnormal positioning.
  • Visuals help the clinic determine how soon your cat needs an appointment.
  • A photo helps the team decide if the extraction should be prioritized.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Retained deciduous teeth are common in dogs and cats due to the failure of the periodontal ligament to detach from the deciduous tooth, often resulting in the permanent tooth erupting in an abnormal position. In cats, this is often observed with canine teeth, where the permanent mandibular canine erupts lingual to the deciduous tooth, creating the appearance of "double fangs". A deciduous tooth should be extracted if it remains firmly attached after the permanent tooth has erupted. If a deciduous tooth lacks a permanent successor, it may be left in place if the roots are strong, provided radiographs confirm the absence of embedded or impacted permanent teeth.

Chapter: Dentistry

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 182)

faqs.question: When should a kitten's baby teeth normally fall out?
faqs.answer: Most kittens lose their deciduous teeth between 3.5 and 6 months of age as their adult teeth begin to erupt.

faqs.question: Will the baby tooth eventually fall out on its own?
faqs.answer: If the adult tooth has fully emerged and the baby tooth is still firmly in place, it is unlikely to fall out on its own and will likely require veterinary extraction.

faqs.question: What happens if I don't get the retained tooth removed?
faqs.answer: Leaving a retained tooth can lead to dental overcrowding, which traps bacteria and debris, eventually causing infection, gum disease, or damage to the permanent tooth.
